occurrence_sentence: MIT physicists have observed the elusive fractional charge effect in five layers of graphene.

They found that when five sheets of graphene are stacked like steps on a staircase, the resulting structure inherently provides just the right conditions for electrons to pass through as fractions of their total charge, with no need for any external magnetic field. Now, MIT physicists have observed the elusive fractional charge effect, this time in a simpler material: five layers of graphene — an atom-thin layer of carbon that stems from graphite and common pencil lead. They report their results today in Nature.

interaction_phrase: study author Long Ju, assistant professor of physics at MIT
occurrence_sentence: "This five-layer graphene is a material system where many good surprises happen," says study author Long Ju, assistant professor of physics at MIT.

interaction_phrase: assistant professor of physics at MIT
occurrence_sentence: "This five-layer graphene is a material system where many good surprises happen," says study author Long Ju, assistant professor of physics at MIT.
That same month, Ju and his team happened to also observe signs of anomalous fractional charge in graphene — a material for which there had been no predictions for exhibiting such an effect.

The researchers fabricated two samples of the hybrid graphene structure by first exfoliating graphene layers from a block of graphite, then using optical tools to identify five-layered flakes in the steplike configuration. They then stamped the graphene flake onto an hBN flake and placed a second hBN flake over the graphene structure. Finally, they attached electrodes to the structure and placed it in the refrigerator, set to near absolute zero. As they applied a current to the material and measured the voltage output, they started to see signatures of fractional charge, where the voltage equals the current multiplied by a fractional number and some fundamental physics constants.

interaction_phrase: supported in part by the Sloan Foundation
occurrence_sentence: This research is supported in part by the Sloan Foundation, and the National Science Foundation.
